title: FINS3646 T1 2026 Assessment Guide
body:
ASSESSMENT GUIDE FINS3646 Toolkit for Finance Term 1, 2026  Assessment Summary Assessment Task % Due Date* Learning Outcomes Assessment 1: Weekly Quizzes (Individual) 15% Weeks 1-10 CLO2 The weekly online quizzes provide you with opportunities to apply the content discussed during the videos, lecture and coding demonstrations. Assessment 2: Project 1 (Individual) 20% Week 5 CLO1, CLO2 In this project, you will creatively combine and apply concepts developed over the first half of the term to solve problems related to the collection, cleaning, and/or analysis of real-world financial data. Assessment 3: Project 2 (Group) 30% Week 10 CLO1, CLO2, In groups, you will combine and apply concepts developed over CLO3, CLO4 the entire term to solve complex problems related to the collection, cleaning, and/or analysis of real-world financial data. You will work as a team, allocating tasks and responsibilities across members, using tools to promote codevelopment of solutions. Final Exam (Individual) 35% Exam CLO2, CLO3 You will be assessed on your understanding of this course and Period knowledge of programming in solving finance problems.

* Due dates are set at Australian Eastern Standard/Daylight Time (AEST/AEDT). If you are in a different time zone, you can use the time and date converter.

Course Learning Outcomes (CLO) CLO1: Develop a toolkit of essential financial market tools applicable to various real-world scenarios. [PLO 1, 2] CLO2: Implement modern techniques to collect, manipulate and analyse financial data. [PLO1, 2] CLO3: Design tools to test predictions derived from financial theory using real world data. [PLO 1, 2] CLO4: Collaborate in technical financial team projects, communicating processes and findings to diverse audiences. [PLO 3, 4]  Assessment Policies Icon legend

Due Date Weighting Format Length/Duration Submission

Late Submissions The parameters for late submissions are outlined in the UNSW Assessment Implementation Procedure. For FINS3646, if you submit your assessments after the due date, you will incur penalties for late submission unless you have Special Consideration (see below). Late submission incurs a 5% penalty per day (including weekends), calculated from the marks allocated to that assessment (not your grade). Assessments will not be accepted more than 5 days late.

Extensions You are expected to manage your time to meet assessment due dates. If you do require an extension to your assessment, please make a request as early as possible before the due date via the special consideration portal on myUNSW (My Student profile > Special Consideration). You can find more information on Special Consideration and the application process below. Lecturers and tutors do not have the ability to grant extensions.

Special Consideration Special consideration is the process for assessing the impact of short-term events beyond your control (exceptional circumstances), on your performance in a specific assessment task. What are circumstances beyond my control? These are exceptional circumstances or situations that may: • Prevent you from completing a course requirement, • Keep you from attending an assessment, • Stop you from submitting an assessment, • Significantly affect your assessment performance.

Available here is a list of circumstances that may be beyond your control. This is only a list of examples, and your exact circumstances may not be listed. You can find more detail and the application form on the Special Consideration site, or in the UNSW Special Consideration Application and Assessment Information for Students.

Referencing • Refer to the student support guide on referencing for assignments: use this link.

Assessment 1: Weekly Quizzes

Weeks 1-10

15%

Online Weekly Quizzes, submitted on Sundays, 11:59pm

Ed platform

CLO2: Implement modern techniques to collect, manipulate and analyse financial data.

Task Description The weekly activities provide opportunities for you to practice the content discussed in each class. There will be a weekly quiz containing a mix of multiple-choice questions, short answer questions, and code challenges.

Instructions • You need to complete all quizzes to receive full credit. • You can submit multiple times before the due date without penalty. Only your last submission will be marked. • Late submissions will be penalised by 5% per day, capped at five days starting immediately after the deadline. After 5 days, submissions will not be accepted and will be recorded as a zero. For example, if you submit your answers 1 minute after the deadline but within 24 hours after, we will deduct 5% from your marks for that week. • Please see the Quizzes slide on the Ed platform for more information.

Submission • Via the Ed platform

Grading and Feedback This assessment consists of: • 8 Weekly Quizzes – 15% evenly allocated across 8 quizzes. o Individual questions and code challenges are categorised into slides on the Ed platform. o Each slide is worth an equal percentage out of 100% o Individual questions or code challenges within a slide are weighted in proportion to the listed point value. o Example: If there are five slides for a quiz, then each slide is worth 20%. If a slide has eight points allocated across the questions, if you lose one point, the slide is worth (7/8) x 20 = 17.5% of the total quiz mark. o Feedback is through Ed, with answers and explanations provided within the assignment slide five days after the due date.

Assessment 2: Project 1 (Individual)

Week 5, Sunday 22 March, 11:59 pm.

20%

Identify, analyse and solve issues in code, explaining your rationale.

Ed platform

CLO1: Develop a toolkit of essential financial market tools applicable to various real-world scenarios. CLO2: Implement modern techniques to collect, manipulate and analyse financial data.

Task Description In this task, you will act as a code reviewer for Python modules that simulate financial data processing tools. These files may include bugs or incomplete logic. Your job is to:

• Diagnose errors, • Fix only what’s broken, and • Explain your reasoning clearly, using data to justify each correction.

This task reflects real-world technical debugging processes used in finance teams, illustrating how AI and collaborative coding work in financial settings. The project develops your problem-solving and communication skills.

What you Need to Do and Deliverables

For each task file (e.g., task_x.py), you must submit the following on Ed:

1. Activity diagram a. A diagram showing how the functions in the module connect. b. Submit as an image or PDF. 2. Corrected Python file (.py) a. Only fix code that is not working properly. b. Leave working code unchanged. 3. Explanation and examples a. Describe every modification to the supplied Python code. b. Use examples from the supplied data to explain why every correction was necessary.

What You’re Not Allowed to Do (The constraints)

• Don’t modify functions that work as expected. • Don’t modify the “main()” functions in any module. • Don’t change function names, signatures, or docstrings.

• Don’t import new libraries. • Don’t add new functions. • Don’t change the config.py module.

AI Use – What’s Allowed?

You may use Generative AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot, Bing Chat) to support your work, but you must:

• Explain how and why you used it in your Explanation (e.g., to debug, clarify logic). • Acknowledge the tool used and the prompt or output (if applicable). • Critically evaluate any AI-generated content. Do not copy without understanding.

Do not use AI to write your final code directly. Undisclosed AI use is misconduct and may result in penalties, including a failing grade or exclusion.

For guidance on ethical use and referencing AI, see: UNSW AI in Teaching Guidelines

Grading and Feedback

Week 7 classes will include a detailed discussion of the project. We will review each function, identifying those that worked as intended and those with bugs. Each bug will be corrected. That is, students who attend class will have a complete understanding of the correct solution. Feedback will include the student’s overall mark as well as summary statistics of the class performance, so that each student can benchmark themselves. These will be provided on Moodle. We will not provide individual feedback and commentary. A thorough discussion of the solution during class time will provide the most comprehensive feedback. Students are invited to ask questions specific to their submission during this time.

Marking Rubric for Assessment 2

Criteria HD Distinction Credit Pass Fail Tips for Improvement Code Correction Adheres to Adheres to Adheres to Adheres to Does not follow Stick to minimal and Fidelity to constraints, fixes constraints; small constraints, some constraints, poor constraints, edits. Only fix what's Constraints only broken unnecessary edits corrections; may fixes introduces errors, broken. Avoid functions, changes may be present have some modifies functions adding code or are effective unnecessary edits that work as changing functions expected, or fails to that work as correct functions expected. that do not. Functional Error Demonstrates Good error Focuses on simple Misses key errors; Little or no error Ask: “What is the Identification & excellent judgment identification with bugs; partial justifications weak identification; lacks function supposed Justification in isolating logical mostly sound rationale provided or unclear rationale or shows to return?” and test errors; includes rationale; handles fundamental with edge cases. clear, well- moderately complex misunderstanding Justify your reasoned issues of the code. changes clearly. justifications Completed (5 marks) Not Completed (0 marks) Activity Diagram Comprehensive, accurate, clearly maps all module functions, Diagrams are missing, severely flawed, or unrelated to program Clarity & Function control flow and decision paths logic. Mapping

Marking Penalty The project page on Ed includes a “validator” to ensure that your code meets important project requirements and can be graded by the course team. Before submitting your code, check if it passes the validator. You will receive a penalty of 20 marks deduction to your overall mark for the project if your submission does not pass the validator in Ed  Assessment 3: Project 2 (Group)

Week 10, Sunday 26 April, 11:59 pm.

30%

Group Programming Project (submitted via Ed platform) Group Size: 2-3 students Ed Platform

CLO1: Develop a toolkit of essential financial market tools applicable to various real-world scenarios. CLO2: Implement modern techniques to collect, manipulate and analyse financial data. CLO3: Design tools to test predictions derived from financial theory using real world data. CLO4: Collaborate in technical financial team projects, communicating processes and findings to diverse audiences.

Task Description In this group-based task, you will work collaboratively to review and debug a series of incomplete or malfunctioning Python modules related to financial data analysis. The assessment simulates the role of a analyst in a fintech setting, requiring both technical and communication skills. Your team must identify logic or functional errors in the code, fix only what is broken, and provide clear justification for each modification. You will reflect on your problem-solving process and teamwork. This task develops your ability to work effectively in technical teams, apply critical thinking, and engage ethically with AI tools.

What’s This Task About?

This assignment simulates a real-world financial coding review. You’ll be provided with Python modules that may contain logical or functional errors. Your role is to test, evaluate, and repair only the parts of the code that are broken. Think like a code reviewer in a fintech team: focus on identifying issues, fixing only what's necessary, and clearly explaining your reasoning.

Students are given incomplete or malfunctioning Python files (task_x.py) related to financial analysis.

Teams will:

1. Test the code and identify logical/data errors. 2. Correct broken code segments without altering functional sections. 3. Explain each change with contextualised reasoning. 4. Reflect on collaboration, debugging processes, and the ethical use of AI.  Groups will be required to reflect on their team’s performance throughout the project by actively conducting an activity called: Stop, Start, Continue at the end of each meeting. As a group you will need to submit this activity as a single PDF file, the activity will demonstrate your group’s ability to meaningfully assess your team’s meeting quality and output by identifying aspects of your meeting that as a group you have agreed to stop, start and/or continue in order to foster more productive future meetings.

You will also need to write an evaluation of you and your team’s performance by identifying areas that you and your group did well and areas that you and the group would like to have improved on for future group work collaborations, you may refer to the Stop, Start, Continue activity to form part of your reflective analysis. Word limit on the written evaluation component is 400 words.

Deliverables

For each .py task file provided, your group must submit the following components on Ed:

1. Corrected Python File (.py) o Only fix code that is not working properly. o Leave working code unchanged 2. Explanation & Examples o Describe every modification to the supplied Python code. o Use examples from the supplied data to explain why every correction was necessary.

You will need to submit your written evaluation on Ed containing your responses to the Stop, Start, Continue reflective activity.

What You’re Not Allowed to Do (The constraints)

• Don’t modify functions that work as expected. • Don’t modify the “main()” functions in any module. • Don’t change function names, signatures, or docstrings. • Don’t import new libraries. • Don’t add new functions. • Don’t change the config.py module.

AI Use – What’s Allowed?

You may use Generative AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot, Bing Chat) to support your work, but you must:

• Explain how and why you used it in your explanation (e.g., to debug, clarify logic). • Acknowledge the tool used and the prompt or output (if applicable). • Critically evaluate any AI-generated content. Do not copy without understanding. • Do not use AI for the reflective analysis or to write your final code directly. • Undisclosed AI use is misconduct and may result in penalties, including a failing grade or exclusion.

For guidance on ethical use and referencing AI, see: UNSW AI in Teaching Guidelines

Grading and Feedback An online session will be held after the course ends (during the study and exam period) that will include a detailed discussion of the project. We will review each function, identifying those that worked as intended and those with bugs. Each bug will be corrected. That is, students who attend class will have a complete understanding of the correct solution. Feedback will include the student’s overall mark as well as summary statistics of the class performance, so that each student can benchmark themselves. These will be provided on Moodle. We will not provide individual feedback and commentary. A thorough discussion of the solution during the online session will yield the most comprehensive feedback. Students are invited to ask questions specific to their submission during this time.

Marking Rubric for Assessment 3

Criteria % HD D C P F Code Correction 80% Effective fixes that follow all Follows constraints with only Some broken functions Incomplete fixes or Breaks working code, Fix broken code constraints; no changes to minor unnecessary edits corrected; a few over- minor violations of ignores constraints, or only; follow all working code edits to working code constraints adds unsupported constraints changes Error Clear, precise explanation of all Strong rationale with mostly Basic justification Justifications vague or Little or no justification; Identification & fixes with data examples; shows clear examples; minor gaps in provided; may lack inconsistent; some major errors missed or Justification strong technical reasoning logic detail or examples fixes not explained misunderstood Explain and justify every change with examples 20% Deep and specific reflection on Strong reflection on group General reflection on Basic or surface-level Lacks reflection or both self and team performance. experience and individual role. team process and own summary of the team contains only vague, Clearly articulates personal Identifies key strengths and participation. Identifies experience. Focuses irrelevant comments. growth, lessons from challenges, weakness of the group and some strengths and or mainly on task No evidence of critical Reflection and how these insights will be connects them to future weaknesses of the completion. Little engagement with the (PLO 4.2) applied to future team or collaboration. Some deeper group, but with limited evidence of learning or team process or workplace settings. Offers analysis of team challenges detail. Future forward thinking. learning outcomes. No critical insight into group and successes is evident. application of learning Minimal insight into future application dynamics and problem-solving is mentioned but not own/team discussed. group issues. well developed. performance.

Marking Penalty

The project page on Ed includes a “validator” to ensure that your code meets important project requirements and can be graded by the course team. Before submitting your code, check if it passes the validator. You will receive a penalty of 20 marks deduction to your overall mark for the project if your submission does not pass the validator in Ed  Assessment 4: Exam Scheduled by UNSW Examinations, announced later in term

35%

Final Exam

In-person via Inspera

CLO2 Implement modern techniques to collect, manipulate and analyse financial data. CLO3 Design tools to test predictions derived from financial theory using real world data

Task Overview The final examination assesses the student’s individual understanding of this course and their knowledge of using programming tools to solve problems in Finance.

The exam is comprehensive, assessing every topic covered in this course. The exam is closed notes and closed book. You will not have access to an IDE or Terminal during the exam. A practice exam will be provided on Ed.

Final Exam Details • Duration: 2 hours • Location: invigilated in-person via Inspera on campus (specific location to be announced later)
